zones.label: I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)
zones.summary: Two-wire serial bus with multi-master, multi-slave support
facts: Uses 2 signal wires: SDA (Serial Data) and SCL (Serial Clock)
Multi-master and multi-slave on one shared bus
Each slave has a unique 7-bit (or 10-bit) address
Speeds: Standard 100 kbps, Fast 400 kbps, Fast Plus 1 Mbps, High Speed 3.4 Mbps
Pull-up resistors required on SDA and SCL
Simple wiring: fewer pins on the MCU
Best for: sensors, RTCs, EEPROMs, and displays
Connectors: Grove (4-pin), JST-PH (4-pin), Qwiic / STEMMA QT (4-pin)

zones.label: SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)
zones.summary: High-speed, full-duplex serial communication for master-slave devices
facts: Uses 4 signal wires: SCK, MOSI, MISO, and CS / SS
Single master; multiple slaves, each with its own CS line
Very high speed: typically up to 10-50 MHz
More pins required on the MCU
No addresses; select a device by pulling its CS line LOW
Best for: displays, SD cards, ADCs, and fast high-speed devices

quiz.question: Which protocol uses only two wires (SDA and SCL) and gives each device a unique address?
quiz.explanation: I2C uses just two wires — SDA (data) and SCL (clock). Every device shares the same two wires and is identified by a unique 7-bit (or 10-bit) address, so up to 127 devices can share one bus.

quiz.question: Which protocol is full-duplex and can run the fastest, often at 10-50 MHz?
quiz.explanation: SPI is full-duplex (it can send and receive at the same time) and runs much faster than I2C — typically 10-50 MHz — which is why it is used for displays and SD cards that need high throughput.

quiz.question: Which protocol requires pull-up resistors on its signal lines?
quiz.explanation: I2C lines are open-drain, so SDA and SCL each need a pull-up resistor to 3.3 V. SPI uses push-pull signals and needs no pull-up resistors.

quiz.question: Which protocol selects a device by pulling its CS line LOW instead of using an address?
quiz.explanation: SPI gives every slave its own Chip Select (CS) wire. You activate a device by driving its CS line LOW, so SPI does not use addresses the way I2C does.
